schema.howto_name: How to Tell If a Crystal Is Real

howto_steps.name: Start with non-destructive tests
howto_steps.text: Temperature, a 10x loupe bubble check, and color zoning — all harmless to the stone.

howto_steps.name: Cross-check with mineral data
howto_steps.text: Compare Mohs hardness, specific gravity, and refractive index against known values for the claimed species.

howto_steps.name: Use price and naming as auxiliary signals
howto_steps.text: Far-below-market pricing or vague naming raises the stakes for further testing.

howto_steps.name: Confirm with a destructive test only if needed
howto_steps.text: The scratch test (Mohs) damages the stone — use only on rough or tumbled quartz-family stones, never on jewelry.

howto_steps.name: For valuable specimens, get a lab report
howto_steps.text: A gemological laboratory certificate is the only definitive answer for high-value stones.

compliance.gentle_note: Crystal identification tests combine practical mineralogy with marketplace awareness. Home tests help you spot common fakes, but for valuable specimens, a gemological lab report is the gold standard. Crystal meanings and energetic properties are separate from authenticity — a stone can be genuinely natural without being a 'healing' tool.
compliance.brand_tagline: We name our stones and sources — return to what's real.
